title: In The Childrenï¿½s Hospital
content:
EMMIE

 I.

Our doctor had callï¿½d in another, I never

had seen him before,

But he sent a chill to my heart when I saw

him come in at the door,

Fresh from the surgery-schools of France

and of other landsï¿½

Harsh red hair, big voice, big chest, big

merciless hands!

Wonderful cures he had done, O, yes, but

they said too of him

He was happier using the knife than in trying

to save the limb,

And that I can well believe, for he lookï¿½d

so coarse and so red,

I could think he was one of those who would

break their jests on the dead,

And mangle the living dog that had loved

him and fawnï¿½d at his kneeï¿½

Drenchï¿½d with the hellish ooraliï¿½that

ever such things should be!



II.

Here was a boyï¿½I am sure that some of

our children would die

But for the voice of love, and the smile,

and the comforting eyeï¿½

Here was a boy in the ward, every bone

seemï¿½d out of its placeï¿½

Caught in a mill and crushï¿½dï¿½it was all

but a hopeless case:

And he handled him gently enough; but his

voice and his face were not kind,

And it was but a hopeless case, he had seen

it and made up his mind,

And he said to me roughly, ï¿½The lad will

need little more of your care.ï¿½

ï¿½All the more need,ï¿½ I told him, ï¿½to seek

The Lord Jesus in prayer;

They are all His children here, and I pray

for them all as my own.ï¿½

But he turnï¿½d to me, ï¿½Ay, good woman,

can prayer set a broken bone?ï¿½

Then he mutterï¿½d half to himself, but I

know that I heard him say,

ï¿½All very wellï¿½but the good Lord Jesus

has had his day.ï¿½



III.

Had? has it come? It has only dawnï¿½d.

It will come by and by.

O, how could I serve in the wards if the

hope of the world were a lie?

How could I bear with the sights and the

loathsome smells of disease

But that He said, ï¿½Ye do it to me, when ye

do it to theseï¿½?



IV.

So he went. And we past to this ward

where the younger children are laid.

Here is the cot of our orphan, our darling,

our meek little maid;

Empty, you see, just now! We have lost

her who loved her so muchï¿½

Patient of pain thoï¿½ as quick as a sensitive

plant to the touch.

Hers was the prettiest prattle, it often

moved me to tears,

Hers was the gratefullest heart I have

found in a child of her yearsï¿½

Nay you remember our Emmie; you used

to send her the flowers.

How she would smile at ï¿½em, play with ï¿½em,

talk to ï¿½em hours after hours!

They that can wander at will where the

works of the Lord are revealï¿½d

Little guess what joy can be got from a

cowslip out of the field;

Flowers to these ï¿½spirits in prisonï¿½ are all

they can know of the spring,

They freshen and sweeten the wards like

the waft of an angelï¿½s wing.

And she lay with a flower in one hand and

her thin hands crost on her breastï¿½

Wan, but as pretty as heart can desire, and

we thought her at rest,

Quietly sleepingï¿½so quiet, our doctor said,

ï¿½Poor little dear,

Nurse, I must do it to-morrow; sheï¿½ll

never live throï¿½ it, I fear.ï¿½



V.

I walkï¿½d with our kindly old doctor as far

as the head of the stair,

Then I returnï¿½d to the ward; the child

didnï¿½t see I was there.



VI.

Never since I was nurse had I been so

grieved and so vext!

Emmie had heard him. Softly she callï¿½d

from her cot to the next,

ï¿½He says I shall never live throï¿½ it; O Annie,

what shall I do?ï¿½

Annie considerï¿½d. ï¿½If I,ï¿½ said the wise

little Annie, ï¿½was you,

I should cry to the dear Lord Jesus to help

me, for, Emmie, you see,

Itï¿½s all in the picture there: ï¿½Little children

should come to meï¿½ï¿½ï¿½

Meaning the print that you gave us, I

find that it always can please

Our children, the dear Lord Jesus with

children about his knees.

ï¿½Yes, and I will,ï¿½ said Emmie, ï¿½but then if

I call to the Lord,

How should he know that itï¿½s me? such a

lot of beds in the ward!ï¿½

That was a puzzle for Annie. Again she

considerï¿½d and said:

ï¿½Emmie, you put out your arms, and you

leave ï¿½em outside on the bedï¿½

The Lord has so much to see to! but, Emmie,

you tell it him plain,

Itï¿½s the little girl with her arms lying out

on the counterpane.ï¿½



VII.

I had sat three nights by the childï¿½I

could not watch her for fourï¿½

My brain had begun to reelï¿½I felt I

could do it no more.

That was my sleeping-night, but I thought

that it never would pass.

There was a thunderclap once, and a clatter

of hail on the glass,

And there was a phantom cry that I heard

as I tost about,

The motherless bleat of a lamb in the

storm and the darkness without;

My sleep was broken besides with dreams

of the dreadful knife

And fears for our delicate Emmie who

scarce would escape with her life;

Then in the gray of the morning it seemï¿½d

she stood by me and smiled,

And the doctor came at his hour, and we

went to see to the child.



VIII.

He had brought his ghastly tools; we believed

her asleep againï¿½

Her dear, long, lean, little arms lying out

on the counterpaneï¿½

Say that His day is done! Ah, why should

we care what they say?

The Lord of the children had heard her,

and Emmie had past away.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
